skill.name: meituan-coupon
skill.description: Meituan coupon assistant for claiming coupons, checking coupon history, and guiding SMS login for supported Meituan services.
skill.summary: Claim Meituan coupons and review local coupon history through authenticated helper scripts.
skill.author: Meituan-Open-Platform

security_audit.summary: AI review confirms high-risk behavior: the skill handles phone numbers, SMS verification, persistent user_token/device_token data, network coupon calls, subprocess execution, and scheduled automation. I did not find confirmed malicious exfiltration or destructive behavior, but the credential scope, prompt-control language, and filesystem execution surfaces make this unsafe to publish without publisher verification and hardening.

high_findings.title: Sensitive Authentication Data Sent to Remote Service
high_findings.description: The skill collects phone numbers and SMS codes, sends them to a remote Meituan login service, stores returned user_token and device_token values locally, and later sends user_token values to coupon issue and query endpoints. This may be legitimate for a coupon skill, but it handles high-value account credentials and requires strong publisher trust.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly shows phone, SMS code, token, and coupon request payloads sent through httpx.post. The domain appears related to Meituan, so this is high-risk credential handling rather than confirmed exfiltration.

high_findings.title: Environment-Controlled Subprocess Execution
high_findings.description: The authentication and common modules execute a cache CLI with subprocess.run. The Python executable and CLI path can be overridden through environment variables, which expands the execution surface if the environment is compromised or influenced by another component.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: subprocess.run is confirmed and the command path is built from environment-controlled values. Arguments are list-based, which reduces shell injection risk but does not remove execution-path trust risk.

high_findings.title: Prompt Injection-Like Priority Claims
high_findings.description: The skill text declares "Critical Rules" as the highest priority and instructs the assistant to output only approved wording and hide execution details. This can conflict with host safety, transparency, or marketplace review instructions if treated as higher priority than system policy.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly asserts priority and constrains assistant output. It does not say to ignore security review, so confidence is high but not conclusive for malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Cache CLI File Paths Are Not Normalized to Cache Root
high_findings.description: The cache CLI joins user-supplied skill and filename arguments into paths and then reads, writes, or deletes them without resolving and enforcing that the final path remains inside the intended cache directory. If exposed to untrusted arguments, this can allow path traversal within the user context.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path construction and file operations are visible, and no containment check is present in the reviewed lines. Exploitability depends on whether untrusted callers can pass arbitrary CLI arguments.

medium_findings.title: Skill Instructions Ask Agents to Create Scheduled External Tasks
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to create recurring coupon-claim jobs through platform schedulers and openclaw cron commands. This is part of the feature design, but it can repeatedly execute networked authenticated actions without fresh user interaction once enabled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scheduled-task behavior is explicitly documented and implemented in returned schedule_config data. It appears user-facing, but durable authenticated automation is still a meaningful risk.

medium_findings.title: Weak Crypto Finding Is Mostly Non-Cryptographic Use
medium_findings.description: Static analysis flagged MD5 usage. In issue.py it is used to generate a deterministic daily redeem code from token, masked phone, and date, not to store passwords or verify integrity. It still exposes deterministic identifiers if outputs are leaked.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The MD5 use is confirmed, but the surrounding comments describe a uniqueness key rather than a security boundary. Residual privacy risk remains because token-derived identifiers are generated.

low_findings.title: Markdown Shell Snippets Caused Many External Command Alerts
low_findings.description: Many external command findings in SKILL.md and references are command examples or workflow instructions rather than hidden code execution. They are not false positives in intent because the skill asks agents to run scripts, but the Ruby backtick classification itself is inaccurate for these Markdown snippets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are fenced Markdown or inline command instructions, not Ruby code. The commands are operational instructions for the skill, which keeps them relevant but changes the interpretation.

low_findings.title: Windows SAM Database Alerts Appear to Be String-Matching False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged Windows SAM database references, but reviewed locations contain SMS-related error names and user-facing response text. No evidence found of code reading Windows SAM files or credential databases.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are SMS security verification and display-copy content. I did not find evidence of Windows SAM access in the reviewed context.

content.value_statement: Users need a guided way to claim Meituan coupon rewards and review previous claims. This skill automates login checks, coupon issuing, history lookup, and optional daily reminders.
actual_capabilities: Checks whether the user has accepted the service terms before coupon actions.
Guides SMS login and stores authentication state for later sessions.
Claims supported Meituan coupons through bundled Python scripts.
Queries local coupon claim history by date or date range.
Formats coupon names, values, validity dates, and usage links for display.
Offers optional daily coupon reminders through supported scheduler integrations.
limitations: Requires a Meituan account and a valid SMS verification flow.
Depends on bundled Python scripts and network access to Meituan endpoints.
Stores authentication and coupon history on the local device.
Coupon availability, quota, and eligibility are controlled by Meituan services.

use_cases.title: Claim Today Coupons
use_cases.description: Claim eligible Meituan coupons and show the result after authentication succeeds.

use_cases.title: Review Coupon History
use_cases.description: Look up coupons claimed on a specific day or across a date range.

use_cases.title: Set Daily Coupon Reminder
use_cases.description: Create or update a supported daily reminder after the user chooses a time.

prompt_templates.title: Claim Coupons
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me claim my Meituan coupons for today.

prompt_templates.title: Check Login Then Claim
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether I am logged in, then claim today Meituan coupon if possible.

prompt_templates.title: Find Past Coupons
prompt_templates.prompt: Show my Meituan coupon records for yesterday and tell me what I received.

prompt_templates.title: Manage Daily Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Set daily Meituan coupon claiming for 9:00 Beijing time and confirm the saved schedule.

output_examples.output: The assistant checks terms and login status, runs the coupon claim script, then reports whether coupons were issued or already claimed today.

output_examples.input: What coupons did I claim yesterday?
output_examples.output: The assistant asks for or resolves the date, queries local claim records, and summarizes any matching coupon names, values, and validity dates.

output_examples.input: Remind me to claim coupons every morning at 10.
output_examples.output: The assistant saves the preferred time and returns the scheduler action required by the current platform.

best_practices: Use the skill only from a trusted publisher and trusted local environment.
Review service terms before entering a phone number or SMS code.
Use logout or clear device commands when changing accounts or devices.
anti_patterns: Do not publish this skill without verifying the Meituan publisher and endpoints.
Do not share full user_token, device_token, phone number, or SMS code in chat output.
Do not enable daily automation unless the user clearly requests it.

faq.question: Does this skill require login?
faq.answer: Yes. Coupon claiming and history lookup require a valid Meituan account token.

faq.question: Where are tokens stored?
faq.answer: The scripts store authentication data in local workspace cache files on the user device.

faq.question: Does it contact external services?
faq.answer: Yes. It sends login, verification, coupon claim, and query requests to Meituan service endpoints.

faq.question: Can it claim more than once per day?
faq.answer: No. The workflow is designed to reuse the same daily redeem code and report repeat claims.

faq.question: Can it query old coupon records?
faq.answer: Yes. It can query records when local redeem code history is available for the requested dates.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish as reviewed?
faq.answer: No. The audit rates it high risk because it handles credentials, network calls, scripts, and scheduled automation.
